title: Trace one durable command
objective: Follow a normal command from CLI arguments to application request to lifecycle coordination to backend boundary.
summary: Trace one durable `sand` command by following responsibility boundaries, not every line of code. The main path is `sand demo run pwd`: entry point composition, router dispatch, request structs, lifecycle orchestration, metadata lookup, working-directory policy, backend delegation, and process exit code.

beats.title: Trace landmarks, not every branch
beats.text: A durable command is a normal command for a named Sandbox VM, such as `sand demo run pwd` or `sand create demo`. Trace it as a handoff chain: executable entry point builds objects, router parses arguments, application boundary receives a typed request, lifecycle code coordinates metadata and policies, then the backend port performs VM work. Stay on one command path. Do not read every `switch` case or backend process detail yet.

beats.title: The composition root
beats.text: `Sources/sand/main.swift` wires the real app together. It creates `FileHostMetadataStore`, `AppleContainerCLIBackend`, `LifecycleCoordinator`, and `CLICommandRouter`. That file is intentionally small: it chooses concrete production implementations, calls `router.dispatch(arguments:)`, then exits with `result.processExitCode`. If dispatch throws, `main.swift` prints `sand: ...` to stderr and exits `1`.

beats.prompt: For `sand demo run pwd`, which object first receives the parsed command-line arguments after `main.swift` drops the executable name?
beats.feedback: `main.swift` constructs the router and calls `router.dispatch(arguments:)`. Backend, metadata, and mapping are reached later through lifecycle orchestration.

beats.title: Router dispatch pattern
beats.text: `CLICommandRouter.dispatch` reads the first argument. Top-level commands like `create` get their own dispatch helpers. Unknown top-level words are treated as sandbox names and routed through `dispatchSandboxFirst`. For `sand demo run pwd`, `demo` becomes `SandboxName`, `run` becomes the action, and `pwd` becomes an opaque `WorkloadCommand`. The router then calls `application.run(RunRequest(...))`.

beats.prompt: Which responsibilities belong to `CLICommandRouter` on this path?

choices.text: Start the Sandbox VM if it is stopped.
Translate host current directory to a Guest Path.

beats.feedback: The router owns command shape and typed requests. Auto-start, working-directory mapping, and backend execution happen after the application boundary.

beats.title: Application boundary
beats.text: `SandboxApplication` is the seam between command parsing and application behavior. It declares methods like `create`, `run`, `status`, `addFolder`, and `ephemeral`, plus request structs such as `CreateRequest`, `RunRequest`, and `NamedSandboxRequest`. The router depends on this protocol, not directly on `LifecycleCoordinator`. Tests use recording applications to prove routing without provisioning a real Sandbox VM.

beats.title: LifecycleCoordinator orchestrates run
beats.text: For `run`, `LifecycleCoordinator` reads the Sandbox Spec from Host Metadata, asks `WorkingDirectoryMapper` to map the Host Mac current directory into a Guest Path, emits a warning if it falls back to `/workspace`, checks backend status, starts the Sandbox VM if stopped, then delegates to `backend.run(BackendRunRequest(...))`. It coordinates; it does not become the backend adapter.

beats.prompt: Predict the high-level calls for `sand demo run pwd` when metadata contains `demo` and the backend reports it is stopped. Ignore low-level process execution.
beats.answer: Router creates `RunRequest`; `LifecycleCoordinator.run` reads the spec, maps the working directory, asks backend for status, calls backend start, then calls backend run with `pwd` and the chosen Guest Path.
beats.explanation: The lifecycle test `testRunAutoStartsStoppedSandboxAndDelegatesOpaqueCommandToBackend` shows the expected backend calls: status, start, run. The command arguments stay opaque all the way to the backend request.

beats.title: Backend port and exit result
beats.text: `SandboxBackend` is the narrow adapter port: readiness, provision, apply, start, stop, run, shell, status, logs, and delete. `LifecycleCoordinator` calls this interface rather than embedding backend command construction. `backend.run` and `backend.shell` return `CommandResult`, so workload exit status can flow back up. `CommandResult.success` maps to process exit `0`; `.failure(exitCode: n)` maps to `n` in `main.swift`.

beats.instruction: Annotate one durable command path. Pick either `sand demo run pwd` or `sand create demo`. Write a numbered trace with file path, object/function, and responsibility for each handoff. Keep it to 8-12 lines; stop at the `SandboxBackend` boundary and the final `CommandResult` exit mapping.
beats.copyText:
Command: sand demo run pwd
1. Sources/sand/main.swift — constructs FileHostMetadataStore, AppleContainerCLIBackend, LifecycleCoordinator, CLICommandRouter.
2. Sources/SandCore/CLI/CLICommandRouter.swift — ...
3. Sources/SandCore/CLI/SandboxApplication.swift — ...
4. Sources/SandCore/Lifecycle/LifecycleCoordinator.swift — ...
5. Sources/SandCore/Metadata/HostMetadataStore.swift — ...
6. Sources/SandCore/WorkingDirectory/WorkingDirectoryMapper.swift — ...
7. Sources/SandCore/Backend/SandboxBackend.swift — ...
8. Sources/SandCore/CLI/SandboxApplication.swift + Sources/sand/main.swift — CommandResult becomes process exit code.
beats.signsOfSuccess: Your trace names the router, request struct, application boundary, lifecycle coordinator, metadata read or write, one relevant policy, backend port, and exit-code conversion. It does not explain every command branch or backend process internals.
hints: For `create`, follow `dispatchCreate` to `CreateRequest`, then metadata `createSpec` and backend `provision`.
Use lifecycle tests as examples of expected backend calls.

beats.recap: A durable command flows through a small set of seams: `main.swift` composes concrete objects, `CLICommandRouter` parses command shape, `SandboxApplication` carries typed requests, `LifecycleCoordinator` coordinates metadata, policies, and runtime state, and `SandboxBackend` is the narrow adapter port. The command's `CommandResult` returns to `main.swift`, where it becomes the process exit code.
beats.readyPrompt: Ready to study the policies inside the flow: Sandbox Specs, Allowed Folders, Access Modes, Guest Paths, and Working Directory Mapping?

referenceNeeds: Add a cheat-sheet trace diagram for `sand demo run pwd`: main.swift → CLICommandRouter.dispatch → dispatchSandboxFirst → RunRequest → LifecycleCoordinator.run → HostMetadataStore.readSpec/currentHostDirectory → WorkingDirectoryMapper.map → SandboxBackend.status/start/run → CommandResult.processExitCode.
Add a practice-bank prompt asking learners to annotate `sand create demo`, including `CreateRequest`, metadata `createSpec`, backend `provision`, rollback on provision error, and exit-code mapping.
